title: Effective Hamiltonians in Cavity and Waveguide QED from Transition-Operator Diagrammatic Perturbation Theory
authors: Mohamed Meguebel, Maxime Federico, Louis Garbe, Nadia Belabas, Nicolas Fabre
abstract: We propose an adiabatic-elimination formalism in the dispersive regime based on a transition-centric perturbation theory. The perturbative expansion is recast into a diagrammatic framework, while adiabatic elimination is implemented through controlled projections onto transition subspaces. Our approach applies systematically at arbitrary perturbation order, and is suited to multilevel systems and multiple qubits in both cavity and waveguide quantum electrodynamics. It ultimately enables the explicit construction of effective higher-order Hamiltonians while bypassing important limitations of existing techniques, thereby providing a practical toolbox for multiphoton processes in the dispersive regime.
summary: This paper introduces a new diagrammatic perturbation theory based on light-matter transition operators to derive effective Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime. It provides a systematic way to handle multi-photon processes and multi-qubit systems in both cavity and waveguide QED. The method is shown to be more computationally efficient than previous techniques for high-order expansions.
main_problem: The need for a systematic, higher-order perturbative framework to derive effective Hamiltonians in the dispersive regime of Cavity and Waveguide QED, specifically for multi-level/multi-qubit systems and continuous frequency spectra.
main_result: The development of a transition-centric diagrammatic perturbation theory (JLM diagrams) that provides a scalable 'toolbox' for constructing higher-order effective Hamiltonians and handles multi-photon processes more efficiently than existing methods.
method: A transition-operator-based diagrammatic expansion using a resolvent method in Laplace space, combined with adiabatic elimination via controlled projections onto transition subspaces.
model_or_system: Hybrid light-matter systems including Cavity QED (discrete modes) and Waveguide QED (continuous modes), involving N-level atoms or N-qubit systems interacting with quantized electromagnetic fields.
key_observables: Effective higher-order Hamiltonians, AC Stark shifts, effective qubit-quibit couplings, and multi-photon transition amplitudes.
important_parameters: Dispersive regime (large detunings), coupling strengths, frequency continua, and regularization parameters for handling divergences.
main_assumptions: The Hamiltonian is assumed to be time-independent, and the expansion is primarily focused on the dispersive regime where coupling-to-detuning ratios are small.
figures_summary: Figure 1 shows the schematic of dispersive expansion and adiabatic elimination; Figure 2 presents the building blocks of JLM diagrams; Figure 3 illustrates 1st and 2nd-order JLM diagrams; Figures 13-16 show the mathematical proof of complexity bounds and a comparison of efficiency against James' method.
paper_structure: The paper introduces the JLM transition operator framework, establishes the diagrammatic rules and perturbative expansion, develops the adiabatic elimination procedure, provides a combinatorial analysis of diagram complexity, and concludes with a comparison of computational efficiency against traditional methods.
why_interesting: It provides a powerful, systematic tool for researchers in quantum optics and circuit QED to analytically derive complex multi-photon interactions and energy shifts in multi-level systems without the computational explosion of standard state-centric approaches.
analysis_basis: full PDF text, analyzed in chunks

FIG. 2. JLM diagrams elementary blocks. States of matter are represented by point vertices labeled by the corresponding ket |·⟩(panel a.). Photon emission and absorption events are indicated by arrows (panel b.). Photon-induced matter tran- sitions are indicated by arrows: absorption events are drawn below the matter axis defined by the matter-state vertices (panel c.), while emission events are drawn above it (panel d.). Free evolution of a JLM transition operator is repre- sented by a free-evolution loop returning to the same matter vertex. When the free evolution follows an absorption (emis- sion) event, the loop is drawn below (above) the matter axis (panels e. and f.). Multiple...

FIG. 6. Schematic illustration of how a JLM diagram (of con- stitutive JLM transition operators indicated in black) and its Hermitian branch (of constitutive JLM transition operators indicated in grey) combine to form a closed loop that returns the system to its initial state. For a given JLM transition op- erator, the Hermitian conjugate carries the opposite detuning. Here, we highlight this for ˆξ and ˆξ† and the same reasoning applies to all other JLM transition operators.
